title-group.article-title: Zinc status following different bariatric procedures: systematic review and meta-analysis
title-group.alt-title: Zinc status following different bariatric procedures

contrib-group.aff: aDepartment of Surgical Oncology, Gansu Provincial Hospital, Lanzhou, China
bDepartment of Anorectal Surgery, Gansu Provincial Hospital, Lanzhou, China

fn.p: These authors contributed equally to this work.
Supplemental data for this article can be accessed online at https://doi.org/10.1080/07853890.2025.2608532.

author-notes.corresp: CONTACT Wenhan Liu 1604107631@qq.com Department of Anorectal Surgery, No. 204, Donggang West Road, Chengguan District, Lanzhou City, Gansu Provincial Hospital, Lanzhou 730000, Gansu Province, China
Chenglou Zhu zhuchenglou@hotmail.com Department of Surgical Oncology, No. 204, Donggang West Road, Chengguan District, Lanzhou City, Gansu Provincial Hospital, Lanzhou 730000, Gansu Province, China.

event.event-desc: published online in a building issue

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Author(s). Published by Informa UK Limited, trading as Taylor & Francis Group

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/), which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. The terms on which this article has been published allow the posting of the Accepted Manuscript in a repository by the author(s) or with their consent.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: This study evaluated perioperative changes in serum zinc levels following different bariatric procedures and provided evidence-based recommendations for postoperative monitoring and supplementation.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: PubMed, Embase, the Cochrane Library, Web of Science and CNKI were systematically searched from inception to July 2025. Eligible studies compared pre- and postoperative serum zinc levels in individuals with obesity undergoing bariatric surgery. Study quality was assessed using the Newcastle–Ottawa Scale (NOS), and the certainty of evidence was graded using the GRADE approach. Pooled analyses were conducted with StataSE 17.0.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Twelve studies including 2,529 participants were analysed, with overall quality rated as high. Compared with baseline, pooled standardized mean differences (SMDs) in serum zinc at 3 months, 6 months, 1 year, and 2 years postoperatively were −0.12 (95% CI: −0.27 to 0.04, I2 = 57.9%, τ2 = 0.0265, p = 0.149), −0.36 (95% CI: −0.58 to −0.14, I2 = 82.2%, τ2 = 0.1043, p = 0.001), −0.35 (95% CI: −0.53 to −0.16, I2 = 81.9%, τ2 = 0.0769, p = 0.001), and −0.36 (95% CI: −0.95 to 0.24, I2 = 97.2%, τ2 = 0.3515, p = 0.240), respectively. Subgroup analysis showed no significant changes at 3 months across procedures. However, zinc levels significantly decreased at 6 and 12 months after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass (RYGB) and mini-gastric bypass (MGB), but not after sleeve gastrectomy (SG). At 2 years, no significant reduction was observed in any group. The certainty of evidence for zinc changes was rated as moderate.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Serum zinc levels decline significantly during the first postoperative year, particularly after RYGB and MGB, while SG shows minimal impact. Routine zinc monitoring and individualized supplementation are recommended within the first year after surgery to prevent deficiency-related complications.

list-item.p: Serum zinc levels in individuals with obesity generally decline following bariatric surgery with the most pronounced reductions occurring at 6 months and 1 year postoperatively.
RYGB and MGB are associated with significant postoperative decreases in serum zinc, whereas SG exerts minimal impact on zinc levels.

When selecting surgical procedures, clinicians should carefully consider the risk of postoperative micronutrient deficiencies and ensure zinc monitoring and supplementation in RYGB and MGB patients to mitigate the risk of related complications.

institution-wrap.institution: Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province

funding-group.funding-statement: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province (grant number 23JRRA1317, 23JRRA1782).

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Obesity is commonly defined as abnormal accumulation of body fat resulting from excessive energy intake or metabolic dysregulation, which leads to increased body weight and a spectrum of pathophysiological disturbances. Studies have shown that obesity is closely associated with cardiovascular and cerebrovascular diseases, type 2 diabetes mellitus, non-alcoholic fatty liver disease, obstructive sleep apnoea syndrome, and various malignancies [1,2]. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), approximately 13% of adults worldwide were classified as having simple obesity in 2016. Obesity and its complications not only seriously impair patients’ physical and mental health and quality of life but also impose substantial burdens on society, the economy, and healthcare systems [3].

Zinc, as an essential trace element, plays an important role in multiple physiological and metabolic processes [4]. It participates in DNA synthesis, cell division, protein synthesis, immune regulation, and wound healing, and is crucial for maintaining the normal function of over 300 enzymes and approximately 1,000 transcription factors [5]. Therefore, fluctuations in serum zinc levels may lead to serious physiological dysfunctions. Clinical manifestations of zinc deficiency are diverse, including hair loss, dermatitis, diarrhoea, nail dystrophy, impaired sexual function, hypogeusia, delayed wound healing, and acrodermatitis enteropathica [6,7].

Zinc absorption primarily depends on specific transport proteins expressed on the membranes of epithelial cells in the duodenum and proximal jejunum [8]. Therefore, in patients undergoing bariatric surgery, particularly those receiving Roux-en-Y gastric bypass (RYGB) or biliopancreatic diversion with duodenal switch (BPD/DS), zinc absorption, distribution, and metabolism are more susceptible to disruption due to alterations in gastrointestinal anatomy and dietary habits. Based on this characteristic, regular monitoring of serum zinc and appropriate supplementation postoperatively are particularly necessary. Currently, both the British Obesity and Metabolic Surgery Society (BOMSS) [7] and the American Society for Metabolic & Bariatric Surgery (ASMBS) [9] recommend postoperative zinc supplementation; the guidelines regarding dosage and monitoring frequency are not standardized, and some recommendations contain inconsistencies or contradictions.

At present, evidence regarding zinc deficiency after bariatric surgery is limited, and the underlying mechanisms and clinical outcomes have not been systematically summarized. Therefore, this study was conducted in accordance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) [10] guidelines to systematically review existing literature. The objectives of this study are to (1) compare changes in serum zinc levels before and after bariatric surgery, (2) evaluate the impact of bariatric surgery on zinc deficiency, and (3) explore differences in these outcomes among various surgical procedures. The ultimate goal is to provide evidence-based guidance for postoperative zinc monitoring and supplementation in clinical practice.

sec.title: Materials and methods
sec.p: This meta-analysis was registered in PROSPERO (CRD420251138846)

sec.title: Literature search
sec.p: A systematic literature search was conducted in PubMed, the Cochrane Library, Web of Science, Embase, and China National Knowledge Infrastructure (CNKI) to identify studies on zinc deficiency after bariatric surgery. The search period ranged from database inception to July 2025. The search strategy combined controlled vocabulary terms (MeSH/Emtree) with free-text terms (Supplementary Table S1). English search terms included: bariatric surgery, gastric bypass, laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy (LSG), Roux-en-Y gastric bypass (RYGB), one-anastomosis gastric bypass (OAGB), Mini-Gastric Bypass (MGB), biliopancreatic diversion with duodenal switch (BPD/DS), zinc, and zinc deficiency.

sec.title: Study selection
sec.p: Two reviewers (ZCL and LWH) independently screened all retrieved records. Titles and abstracts were first evaluated to exclude clearly irrelevant studies, followed by full-text review to determine eligibility based on predefined inclusion and exclusion criteria. Discrepancies were resolved through discussion or consultation with a third reviewer.

sec.title: Inclusion and exclusion criteria
sec.p: Inclusion criteria: (1) Population: individuals with obesity undergoing bariatric surgery, regardless of region, sex, age, or preoperative zinc levels, and reporting pre- and postoperative serum zinc levels; (2) Study design: clinical trials, cohort studies, or retrospective studies; (3) Outcome measures: postoperative serum zinc levels reported as mean ± standard deviation; (4) Full-text articles with complete data. Exclusion criteria: studies involving postoperative nutritional supplementation that could affect zinc outcomes; duplicate publications; studies with incomplete outcome data that could not be obtained from the authors; animal studies, reviews, meta-analyses, case reports, conference abstracts, theses, letters, or commentary articles.

sec.title: Data extraction and quality assessment
sec.p: Data were extracted using a standardized form, including: (1) study characteristics: first author, year of publication, country, study design, and follow-up duration; (2) baseline characteristics: sample size, sex distribution, mean age, BMI, type of surgery, preoperative zinc level; (3) outcome measures: postoperative serum zinc levels. To ensure consistency, serum zinc levels reported in different units were converted to μg/dL. Given that all included studies were observational, methodological quality was assessed using the Newcastle–Ottawa Scale (NOS) [11]. The NOS evaluates three domains: selection of study population (4 items, 1 point each), comparability (1 item, up to 2 points), and outcome/exposure assessment (3 items, 1 point each), with a maximum score of 9 points. Two reviewers independently performed the assessment, and discrepancies were resolved through discussion.

sec.title: Certainty of evidence assessment
sec.p: The certainty (quality) of the evidence for each key outcome was evaluated using the GRADE (Grading of Recommendations, Assessment, Development, and Evaluation) methodology. Two reviewers independently assessed the certainty of evidence across the following five domains: risk of bias, inconsistency, indirectness, imprecision, and publication bias. Evidence from observational studies was initially rated as low certainty and could be downgraded for limitations in study design, heterogeneity, or imprecision, or upgraded for large effect size, dose–response relationships, or residual confounding that would likely reduce the observed effect. Disagreements between reviewers were resolved by discussion or, when necessary, through consultation with a third senior investigator. The final ratings were categorized as high, moderate, low, or very low certainty.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: All statistical analyses were conducted using StataSE version 17.0. Changes in serum zinc levels before and after surgery were evaluated using means and standard deviations, while the prevalence of zinc deficiency was assessed using rates with 95% confidence intervals (CIs). Between-study heterogeneity was assessed using the I² statistic: I²<25% indicates low heterogeneity, 25–50% indicates mild, 50–75% indicates moderate, and >75% indicates high heterogeneity. A fixed-effects model was applied when heterogeneity was low or moderate, and a random-effects model was used for high heterogeneity. If fewer than three studies were available, I² was not calculated. Subgroup analyses were conducted according to the type of surgical procedure, continent, study design, baseline zinc level, mean BMI, and mean age. If the number of included studies >10, publication bias was evaluated using funnel plots, if not, the Egger and Begg tests are utilized. Sensitivity analyses were performed by sequentially excluding individual studies.

sec.title: Literature screening process and results
sec.p: A total of 386 records were identified through systematic database and manual searches. After removing duplicates, 173 records remained. Screening of titles and abstracts excluded irrelevant studies, leaving 34 articles for full-text review. Of these, 6 studies were excluded due to the following reasons: one was a review without original data, two lacked specific zinc outcome data, one did not report baseline zinc levels, one study was not relevant to the research topic; and one study was a duplicate publication. Ultimately, 12 studies [12–23] met the inclusion criteria. The literature screening process is summarized in Figure 1.

caption.p: PRISMA flow diagram of the meta-analysis.

sec.title: Baseline characteristics and quality Assessment of included studies
sec.p: Twelve clinical studies published between 2007 and 2024 in Brazil, Switzerland, France, Taiwan (China), Iran, Qatar, and Saudi Arabia were included. All studies were prospective or retrospective cohort designs, with follow-up durations ranging from 3 to 24 months. A total of 2,529 individuals with obesity were included, with sample sizes ranging from 22 to 413. The mean age of participants was primarily in the young to middle-aged adult range (approximately 16.5–49.3 years). Surgical procedures included SG, RYGB, MGB, and OAGB, with SG and RYGB being the most commonly applied; some studies included multiple types of surgery. Patients’ BMI ranged from 39 to 57 kg/m², indicating moderate to severe obesity. Baseline serum zinc levels ranged from 76.9 to 118.9 μg/dL (Tables 1,2). All studies were assessed using the Newcastle–Ottawa Scale (NOS), and overall study quality was high (scores 7–8). Detailed itemized scoring for each study is presented in Supplementary Table S2. Two reviewers independently rated each study with discrepancies resolved through discussion or consultation with a third senior reviewer.

fn.p: N, number of participants; M, male; F, female; RYGB, Roux-en-Y gastric bypass; BMI, body mass index; SG sleeve gastrectomy; OAGB one-anastomosis gastric bypass; MGB, Mini-Gastric Bypass; NOS, Newcastle-Ottawa Quality Assessment Scale.

caption.p: Postoperative zinc deficiency levels in included studies.

tr.th: Serum zinc levels 3 months after surgery(μg/dL)
Serum zinc levels 6 months after surgery(μg/dL)
Serum zinc levels 12 months after surgery (μg/dL)
Serum zinc levels 24 months after surgery (μg/dL)

fn.p: RYGB, Roux-en-Y gastric bypass; BMI, body mass index; SG sleeve gastrectomy; OAGB one-anastomosis gastric bypass; MGB, Mini-Gastric Bypass.

sec.title: Overall effect of bariatric surgery on serum zinc levels
sec.p: Compared with baseline, serum zinc levels in individuals with obesity generally declined over time after bariatric surgery. Pooled analyses using a random-effects model showed: at 3 months postoperatively, serum zinc levels slightly decreased but without statistical significance (SMD = −0.12, 95% CI: −0.27 to 0.04, I2 = 57.9%, τ2 = 0.0265, p = 0.149)(Figure 2); at 6 months, levels significantly decreased (SMD = −0.36, 95% CI: −0.58 to −0.14, I2 = 82.2%, τ2 = 0.1043, p = 0.001)(Figure 3); at 1 year, the downward trend persisted (SMD = −0.35, 95% CI: −0.53 to −0.16, I2 = 81.9%, τ2 = 0.0769, p = 0.001)(Figure 4); and at 2 years, the decrease was no longer statistically significant (SMD = −0.36, 95% CI: −0.95 to 0.24, I2 = 97.2%, τ2 = 0.3515, p = 0.240)(Figure 5). These results indicate that serum zinc levels decline most noticeably in the short- to mid-term (6 months to 1 year) and tend to stabilize during long-term follow-up (2 years).

caption.p: Forest plot showing the effect of bariatric surgery on zinc levels in patients with obesity at 3 months post-surgery(pooled SMD = −0.12 [95% CI: −0.27 to 0.04], I2 = 57.9%, τ2 = 0.0265, p = 0.149).
Forest plot showing the effect of bariatric surgery on zinc levels in patients with obesity at 6 months post-surgery(pooled SMD = −0.36 [95% CI: −0.58 to −0.14], I2 = 82.2%, τ2 = 0.1043, p = 0.001).
Forest plot showing the effect of bariatric surgery on zinc levels in patients with obesity at 1 year post-surgery(pooled SMD = −0.35 [95% CI: −0.53 to −0.16], I2 = 81.9%, τ2 = 0.0769, p = 0.001).

Forest plot showing the effect of bariatric surgery on zinc levels in patients with obesity at 2 years months post-surgery(pooled SMD = −0.36 [95% CI: −0.95 to 0.24], I2 = 97.2%, τ2 = 0.3515, p = 0.240).

sec.title: Impact of different bariatric procedures on serum zinc levels
sec.p: Subgroup analysis revealed significant differences in the effects of different surgical procedures on postoperative serum zinc levels. Overall, pooled results showed significant decreases at 6 months (SMD = −0.36, 95% CI: −0.58 to −0.14, I2 = 82.2%, τ2 = 0.1043, p = 0.001) (Figure 3) and 1 year (SMD = −0.35, 95% CI: −0.53 to −0.16, I2 = 81.9%, τ2 = 0.0769, p = 0.001) (Figure 4), whereas no significant differences were observed at 3 months (SMD = −0.12, 95% CI: −0.24 to 0.04, I2 = 57.9%, τ2 = 0.0265, p = 0.149) (Figure 2) or 2 years (SMD = −0.36, 95% CI: −0.95 to 0.24, I2 = 97.2%, τ2 = 0.3515, p = 0.240) (Figure 5). Analysis by surgical type showed that SG patients did not experience significant decreases in serum zinc levels at any postoperative time point, suggesting minimal impact on zinc metabolism (Figures 2–5). In contrast, RYGB patients exhibited significant reductions at 6 months (SMD = −0.45, 95% CI: −0.72 to −0.17, I2 = 72.9%, τ2 = 0.0788, p = 0.002) (Figure 3) and 1 year (SMD = −0.25, 95% CI: −0.42 to −0.07, I2 = 56.7%, τ2 = 0.0244, p = 0.006) (Figure 4), indicating a higher risk of zinc deficiency during mid-term follow-up. MGB had an even greater effect, with significant decreases at 6 months (SMD = −0.88, 95% CI: −1.57 to −0.19, I2 = 72.8%, τ2 = 0.1933, p = 0.013) (Figure 3) and 1 year (SMD = −0.58, 95% CI: −0.74 to −0.41, p < 0.05) (Figure 4), with larger reductions than RYGB, suggesting a higher postoperative risk of zinc deficiency. Evidence for OAGB was limited, with some indication of decreased serum zinc at 12 months, but more studies are needed to confirm this. Overall, gastric bypass procedures (RYGB, MGB, OAGB) had greater impacts on serum zinc levels, whereas SG appeared relatively safe, highlighting the differences in nutritional outcomes among surgical types.

To facilitate clinical interpretation, SMDs were converted to approximate absolute mean changes in serum zinc concentration (µg/dL) using the pooled baseline standard deviation of zinc levels (≈18 µg/dL across included studies). The overall SMDs corresponded to the following estimated mean decreases: At 6 months: SMD = −0.36 ≈ −6.5 µg/dL, At 12 months: SMD = −0.35 ≈ −6.3 µg/dL. Given that the normal serum zinc reference range is 70–120 µg/dL and that clinical zinc deficiency is generally defined as <70 µg/dL (WHO, NIH, and BOMSS guidelines), these absolute changes represent modest declines, unlikely to induce overt deficiency in individuals with normal baseline levels. However, for patients with preoperative zinc concentrations near the lower end of normal (≈75–80 µg/dL), such reductions may shift values below the deficiency threshold, potentially predisposing them to subclinical or mild deficiency.

sec.title: Subgroup analyses
sec.p: Subgroup analyses were performed according to continent, study design, baseline zinc level, mean BMI, and mean age to explore the sources of heterogeneity at each follow-up time point (3, 6, 12, and 24 months) (Supplementary Table S3). Collectively, subgroup analyses demonstrated that the most significant reductions in serum zinc occurred within the first postoperative year, particularly among patients with higher BMI, lower baseline zinc, and older age. However, heterogeneity remained high (I2> 50%) across most subgroups, indicating that unmeasured factors such as assay variability, and study design differences likely contributed to the observed variability.

sec.title: Publication bias and sensitivity analysis
sec.p: Publication bias was assessed using funnel plots and Egger’s and Begg’s regression tests for each time point. The number of studies included in each funnel analysis was 9 (3 months), 12 (6 months), 12 (12 months), and 4 (24 months), respectively. For all outcomes, both Egger’s and Begg’s tests yielded p values greater than 0.05. However, because the number of studies per analysis was less than 10 for most outcomes, these tests have limited statistical power, and the absence of significant results should not be interpreted as definitive evidence of no publication bias. Visual inspection of the funnel plots suggested approximate symmetry, but small-study effects could not be excluded. Sensitivity analyses conducted by sequentially excluding individual studies at each follow-up time point (3, 6, 12, and 24 months) did not substantially alter the results, indicating the overall stability of this meta-analysis (Supplementary Figure S1–S4).

sec.title: Certainty of evidence (GRADE assessmevnt)
sec.p: The certainty of evidence for changes in serum zinc levels after bariatric surgery was assessed using the GRADE approach. Overall, the evidence derived from observational studies was initially rated as low certainty but could be upgraded or downgraded based on study quality, consistency, precision, and magnitude of effect. The certainty of evidence varied by follow-up duration. At 3 months, the certainty was rated as low due to imprecision and small sample sizes, and the overall effect showed no significant change compared with baseline. At 6 months, the certainty was upgraded to moderate, reflecting a consistent and clinically relevant decline in serum zinc, particularly following RYGB and MGB procedures. At 12 months, the evidence remained moderate, as reductions in zinc levels were consistent across studies and of similar magnitude. In contrast, the 24-month outcome was rated as low certainty because of the limited number of studies and wide confidence intervals. Taken together, these findings indicate moderate-certainty evidence that serum zinc levels decline significantly within the first postoperative year after gastric bypass surgery, emphasizing the need for routine zinc monitoring and individualized supplementation during this period (Supplementary Table S4).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Zinc is an essential trace element required for maintaining cellular function and metabolism, and its deficiency is closely associated with obesity and related metabolic abnormalities [24]. Dysregulation of zinc homeostasis may contribute to obesity through multiple mechanisms, including increased oxidative stress, chronic low-grade inflammation, and impaired adipose tissue secretory function[25–27]. Zinc modulates levels of zinc-α2-glycoprotein (ZAG) and leptin, thereby influencing lipolysis, inflammatory cytokine expression, and leptin signaling pathways, which can create a vicious cycle exacerbating obesity and metabolic dysfunction [26]. Several studies have reported decreased serum zinc levels in individuals with obesity, accompanied by increased urinary zinc excretion, which negatively correlates with body mass index (BMI) and waist circumference, highlighting zinc’s potential role in obesity pathophysiology [28,29].

In the present study, 12 clinical studies published between 2007 and 2024 were systematically analysed to evaluate the impact of bariatric surgery on serum zinc levels. Overall, serum zinc levels exhibited a time-dependent decline postoperatively, with the most pronounced reductions occurring between 6 months and 1 year, whereas changes at 3 months and 2 years were not statistically significant. These findings suggest that the mid-term postoperative period may represent a high-risk phase for zinc deficiency, emphasizing the need for intensified monitoring and timely intervention during this period. Subgroup analysis demonstrated that different surgical procedures exerted distinct effects on postoperative zinc levels. SG patients did not exhibit significant decreases at any follow-up time points, indicating minimal interference with zinc absorption and metabolism. In contrast, RYGB and MGB patients showed significant reductions at 6 months and 1 year, with MGB demonstrating a greater magnitude of decline, suggesting that gastric bypass procedures, particularly MGB, may increase the risk of postoperative zinc deficiency. Evidence for OAGB was limited, with some indication of decreased serum zinc at 12 months, although data remain insufficient. Overall, the type of surgical procedure is a key determinant of postoperative zinc changes, with gastric bypass procedures causing greater disruption to zinc metabolism, whereas SG appears relatively safe. These results provide guidance for preoperative surgical selection and postoperative nutritional management. Compared with previous studies, our findings are consistent with multiple single-center or small-sample reports that suggested a higher incidence of zinc deficiency after RYGB, while SG patients showed minimal changes. This study further corroborates these observations through systematic integration of multicentre data. Moreover, it is the first meta-analysis to quantify serum zinc changes at specific postoperative time points, offering a quantitative basis for clinical monitoring schedules. Zinc deficiency often lacks obvious clinical symptoms, with only a few patients exhibiting impaired immune function, hair loss, taste disturbances, or renal dysfunction [30]. This underscores the importance of routine postoperative serum zinc monitoring to enable early identification of at-risk individuals.

The mechanisms underlying zinc deficiency after bariatric surgery include insufficient intake, bypass of major absorption sites, and reduced intestinal absorption capacity [8]. Evidence indicates that even routine postoperative supplementation with 7.5–15 mg/day of zinc may be insufficient to prevent deficiency, whereas higher doses (15–30 mg/day) can effectively increase serum zinc levels. However, supplementation should not exceed the tolerable upper limit of 40 mg/day to avoid interference with the absorption of other trace elements such as iron and copper [31,32]. Dietary sources rich in zinc include dairy products, red meat, poultry, and nuts, whereas phytates, fiber, and high-calcium foods can reduce zinc absorption [33,34]. Postoperative dietary management should ensure adequate high-quality protein intake (recommended 60–80 g/day) to facilitate zinc absorption and utilization, with zinc supplementation adjusted according to surgical type and individual patient factors [35].

Although statistically significant declines in serum zinc levels were observed at 6 and 12 months after bariatric surgery, the magnitude of change (approximately 5–7 µg/dL) appears relatively small in absolute terms. This suggests that, on average, postoperative zinc decreases are biochemically measurable but of uncertain clinical relevance in individuals with sufficient preoperative zinc status. Nevertheless, patients with marginal baseline levels may cross the deficiency threshold (<70 µg/dL), especially after gastric bypass procedures (RYGB and MGB), where malabsorption and reduced dietary intake compound the risk. Clinically meaningful zinc deficiency is typically associated with symptoms such as dermatitis, alopecia, taste alteration, delayed wound healing, and immune dysfunction [30]. None of the included studies systematically reported such outcomes, underscoring the need for future research linking biochemical reductions to clinical endpoints. Therefore, while the current findings indicate a moderate biochemical decline, the clinical significance remains uncertain, and postoperative zinc monitoring is warranted, particularly for high-risk patients undergoing bypass surgery.

Based on these findings, routine monitoring of serum zinc and long-term, appropriate supplementation are recommended for bariatric surgery patients, particularly those undergoing MGB and RYGB. Postoperative dietary management should emphasize high-quality protein and zinc-rich foods, combined with multivitamin supplementation, to enhance zinc bioavailability. These measures may help reduce the risk of postoperative metabolic disorders, immune dysfunction, and related complications.

This study has several limitations that should be considered when interpreting the results. First, the included studies were primarily prospective and retrospective cohort studies, some single-center, with varying sample sizes, which may introduce selection bias and statistical heterogeneity. Second, although subgroup analyses by surgical type and follow-up time were conducted, the limited number of studies for certain procedures (e.g. OAGB) results in insufficient evidence, affecting the generalizability of conclusions. Third, differences in zinc assay methods, laboratory standards, and postoperative supplementation across studies may affect measurement and comparability of serum zinc levels. Fourth, follow-up durations ranged from 3 to 24 months, with limited long-term data, preventing assessment of zinc changes and clinical outcomes beyond 2 years. Finally, despite Egger’s and Begg’s tests yielded non-significant results (p > 0.05), these tests are known to be unreliable when the number of studies per outcome is small (<10), as in the present meta-analysis. Therefore, the possibility of publication bias cannot be completely ruled out, and the interpretation of funnel plot symmetry should be made with caution. Future updates including additional studies may help clarify the true extent of small-study effects and publication bias.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: After bariatric surgery, serum zinc levels in individuals with obesity exhibit a clear time-dependent decline, with the most pronounced reductions occurring during mid-term follow-up (6 months to 1 year postoperatively). The impact of different surgical procedures on zinc levels varies: SG has minimal interference, whereas gastric bypass procedures (RYGB and MGB) are more likely to cause zinc deficiency, with MGB showing the greatest effect. These findings indicate that the mid-term postoperative period represents a high-risk phase for zinc deficiency, highlighting the need for intensified serum zinc monitoring and individualized nutritional interventions to prevent deficiency-related complications. Moreover, the results provide evidence-based guidance for preoperative surgical selection, postoperative follow-up management, and trace element supplementation, while also offering foundational data for future multicenter studies with long-term follow-up.

sec.title: Author’s contributions
sec.p: CRediT: Chenglou Zhu: Conceptualization, Data curation, Software, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing; Wenhan Liu: Data curation, Methodology, Resources, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

sec.title: Statement of informed consent
sec.p: Not applicable. This article does not require informed consent because the study data were downloaded from the open database. This is a secondary analysis of data from previous studies.

sec.title: Ethical approval
sec.p: Not applicable. This article did not require an ethical board approval because the study data were downloaded from the open database. This is a secondary analysis of data from previous studies.

sec.title: Disclosure statement
sec.p: No potential conflict of interest was reported by the authors.

sec.title: Data availability
sec.p: Some or all data, models, or code generated or used during the study are available from the corresponding author by request.
